subject: science
title: science_7.json
description: Grade 4 Science Assessment - Day 7: Plant Structures and Adaptations

questions.q: What is the primary role of a flower on a plant?
options: To absorb nutrients from the soil
To breathe in carbon dioxide gas
To anchor the plant to the ground
questions.hint: Think about why flowers attract bees and butterflies with their bright colors.
questions.explanation: The flower contains the reproductive organs of a plant, designed to produce seeds that will grow into new plants.

questions.q: Which part of a plant cell contains the green pigment chlorophyll and acts as the site for food production?
questions.hint: This tiny structure acts like a solar panel inside the leaf cell.
questions.explanation: Chloroplasts are the organelles within plant cells where photosynthesis takes place, capturing light energy using chlorophyll.

questions.q: What is the name of the process where a seed begins to sprout and grow into a young plant?
questions.hint: It happens after a seed absorbs enough water and warmth in the soil.
questions.explanation: Germination is the early stage of plant development when a seed awakens from dormancy and starts growing roots and shoots.

questions.q: Why do some rainforest plants have large, wide leaves while desert plants like cacti have sharp needles?
options: Wide leaves capture maximum sunlight in shady forests, while needles reduce water loss in dry deserts.
Wide leaves repel insects, while needles absorb underground water vapor.
Rainforest plants have too much soil nutrition, making their leaves swell up.
Desert needles grow wide at night to catch heavy tropical downpours.
questions.answer: Wide leaves capture maximum sunlight in shady forests, while needles reduce water loss in dry deserts.
questions.hint: Think about how the environment dictates what a plant needs to survive or save.
questions.explanation: In a dark rainforest floor, wide leaves catch limited sunlight. In a desert, needles (modified leaves) have minimal surface area to prevent water from evaporating.

questions.q: What do we call the male reproductive powder produced by the anther of a flower?
questions.hint: Bees collect this yellow dust while moving from bloom to bloom.
questions.explanation: Pollen grains carry the male plant cells required to fertilize the female ovules inside a flower.

questions.q: What sweet liquid do flowers produce to attract pollinators like birds and butterflies?
questions.hint: Bees turn this sugary fluid into honey back at their hive.
questions.explanation: Nectar is a sugary fluid secreted by plants within flowers to encourage insects and animals to assist with cross-pollination.

questions.q: Which of the following is a non-flowering plant that reproduces using spores instead of seeds?
questions.hint: Look closely at the underside of their feathery green leaves for tiny brown dots.
questions.explanation: Ferns and mosses are vascular plants that do not produce flowers or seeds; instead, they reproduce via tiny structures called spores.

questions.q: What is the function of a plant's stem?
options: To support leaves and transport water and nutrients throughout the plant
To absorb sunlight directly into the underground root systems
To keep the plant warm during freezing winter nights
To turn into a fruit once pollination is complete
questions.answer: To support leaves and transport water and nutrients throughout the plant
questions.hint: It acts like the main backbone and plumbing system of the plant above ground.
questions.explanation: The stem structurally holds up the plant parts and contains vascular tissues (xylem and phloem) to move fluids.

questions.q: A plant is placed in a window box, and over two weeks, its stem bends sharply toward the glass. What is this growth response to light called?
questions.hint: The prefix 'photo-' means light, and '-tropism' means turning or moving toward.
questions.explanation: Phototropism is a plant growth mechanism where cells farthest from the light elongate, causing the stem to curve toward the light source.

questions.q: What microscopic pores on the underside of leaves allow gases to enter and exit?
questions.hint: They act like tiny mouths that open and close to control water loss and breathing.
questions.explanation: Stomata are specialized microscopic openings on leaves that regulate gas exchange and the release of water vapor.
